nodes.label: Retired Netflix Engineering Director On Regrets, Video Engineering, Hiring Stories
nodes.guest_role: Retired Netflix engineering director, later Meta director and principal engineer (video infrastructure)
takeaways: Netflix's 'no brilliant jerks' rule was revolutionary in the early 2000s; companies survive the departure of 'indispensable' difficult heroes just fine, as Ronca saw when a hoarding hero engineer quit and everyone else simply moved in.

Patty McCord told him in his own Netflix interview that they don't value 24/7 work ('blow us away with what you can do in an eight hour day'); Ronca later forced a burned-out engineer to take vacation regardless of outages, and the team stabilized the system precisely because they stepped back.

Netflix's flat single-level system and 'personal top of market' compensation (match any competing offer) worked for a small all-senior team but broke at scale: without leveling nobody could rationalize pay gaps, and without individual credit the best engineers eventually leave.

Leetcode 'does not tell you anything about an engineer'; the innate foundation of all engineering is understanding complex systems, technical intuition, and making good decisions without enough data. He successfully hired a train-automation engineer and a wastewater-treatment civil engineer (whose plant was a complex finite state machine) into video software roles on that basis.
When in doubt take the lower level at big tech: come in as a 5 executing like a 6 and bonuses, multipliers and RSU refreshers follow; come in over-leveled and 'there's no way to fix that problem.'

The strongest engineer he ever worked with is Ioannis 'Giannis' Katsavounidis, ex signal-processing professor, whose work with Ronca birthed content-based encoding and later convex hull encoding, now the encoding model at both Netflix and Meta.

Joining Meta as an outside director over a well-functioning ~60-person video org, he was told two weeks in: 'you've been here two weeks, you're already telling us what we need to do.' Lesson: build trust first; if a team executes well, first don't break it, then help it move forward.

From April 2020, Facebook video ran at New Year's Eve peak load every day; his org fixed race conditions in systems never designed for that sustained load while 3.5B users (vs Netflix's ~300M) depended on the apps.

InterVideo's 24/7 startup culture (10am to 10pm-or-3am, seven days a week) ended in a stage-3 colon cancer diagnosis in 2004 with a 25% five-year prognosis; his advice is to bake work-life balance into your DNA early — crunch must be the exception, not the rule.

The most valuable thing Meta taught him was objective performance management: data-driven calibrations over who gets credit and PSC packets permanently recording individual achievement — exactly what Netflix's team-first culture lacked.

His formative book is '12 Secrets to Microsoft Success' (betting the company, good failure, dogfooding, hiring the top 5%, Bill Gates's deliberate hiring freeze); he argues its lessons predicted the 2021 FAANG overhiring and 2022 contraction.

Advice to his younger self: don't waste years coasting at large failing companies; work on hard problems ('if your job is not difficult, you're not growing'), work for visionary leaders who can execute, and start financial planning on day one.

quotes.speaker: David Ronca (quoting Patty McCord)
quotes.quote: We don't value 24/7 work here and if you just come in and work all the time, we're not going to be impressed... if you want to impress us, blow us away with what you can do in an eight hour day.

quotes.speaker: David Ronca
quotes.quote: If this company cannot survive without you here on the clock, we got a problem. You need to take a vacation. And I don't care what happens, if things break, they break.

quotes.speaker: David Ronca
quotes.quote: When you see a brilliant engineering mind, everything else doesn't matter... Because these things can all be learned. They can be learned in a very short time. But you can't teach that.

quotes.speaker: David Ronca
quotes.quote: If it's a broken team, your job is to unbreak it. But if it's a team that's actually executing well, your job is to not break it first and then help it move forward second.

nodes.label: Meta Distinguished Eng (IC9) On Influencing Engs, Failures, and Learnings
nodes.guest_role: Distinguished Engineer (IC9) at Meta, iOS infrastructure
takeaways: Ernst ran a software company in middle school (Cosmic Soft), selling REALbasic online-testing software to teachers worldwide via a proto-Stripe and $19.95 mailed checks.
He joined Facebook weeks before the 2012 IPO as an E5, when all iOS engineers fit in one conference room, during the native rewrite away from HTML5.

His E6 promo came from replacing Apple's Core Data (which 'falls over completely' beyond a small team) with MemModels, an immutable model system, over pushback from engineers loyal to vanilla Apple frameworks that don't scale to Meta's size.

His influence-without-authority playbook: talk in person, open by sympathizing with the other side, do deep research (his team disassembled Core Data's closed source to prove why it couldn't work), and 'do the work for them' so teams only have to say yes.

Code review is undervalued: he reviewed 1,600+ diffs in a half (~14 per workday) as an organic, viral channel of influence; good comments explain why rather than dictating, stay flexible, and invite missing context.

ComponentKit (his IC7 promo project, 2014) brought React concepts to iOS before Swift, SwiftUI, or React Native existed, at the nudge of manager Lee Byron (GraphQL co-inventor); adoption required concrete code comparisons, performance data, heavyweight allies, senior mediators, and compromise with the rival Panels framework.

ComponentScript, his two-year cross-platform framework, was 'a total and complete failure' despite checking every technical box: it targeted no specific engineer persona, he refused to compromise on GraphQL data consistency while a server-driven rival skipped it and won, and he went wide instead of landing one committed anchor team. Lesson: technically excellent doesn't mean it will win.

A Meets Most rating and a fresh-eyed new manager forced the cancellation he had avoided for a year despite gut-level sleepless nights; his coding-machine reflex of 'just write more code' was the wrong response to a failing project.

He killed ComponentScript responsibly — migrating every feature off it, deleting the framework from the repo, and publishing a candid public postmortem — and believes he gained more goodwill than he lost.
He never wanted management: he loves writing code, is weaker at docs and alignment, and communicates best when anchored to concrete technical problems.

His depth-plus-breadth method: when blocked by another team's system, dive 'eight levels deep' into its guts and show up with the diagnosis instead of a ticket — impressing owners, saving their time, and organically accumulating cross-system expertise.

On IC9 expectations he has 'flipped the switch off': he checks with his manager that he's solving problems that matter to the org, then works without meta-anxiety, treating the worry itself as counterproductive.

The engineers he admires span archetypes: coding machine Dustin Shahidipour, fixer Wei Han, project-starter Michael Bolin (Buck, Miles), communicators Bob Baldwin and Oliver Ricard, and alignment-driver Nolan O'Brien who manages the Meta-Apple relationship.
His only career advice: do what you love — specifically, like solving problems — because enthusiasm can't be faked and output, curiosity, and deep digging are all downstream of it.

nodes.label: Instagram Principal Eng (IC8) On Building IG Stories, 1 Promo Per Half, Small Teams
nodes.guest_role: Instagram principal engineer (IC8), led iOS for Instagram Stories; co-founder of Retro
takeaways: Olson failed his 2011 Facebook internship interview so badly the interviewer cut it short and said 'you're just not gonna be able to make it at Facebook' — he later reached IC8 there, and for full-time interviews used prescribed beta blockers to break the physical nerves feedback loop.

At ~50-person Flipboard he interned alongside future billionaires Dylan Field (Figma) and Devin Finzer (OpenSea); what marked them out was determination to found companies and conviction in a technical insight (WebGL) they iterated toward product-market fit.

His open-source iOS debugging tool FLEX got him the Instagram job — interviewers refused to look at it, but engineer Jonathan Dan saw it, pulled him into the pipeline, and advocated for him after a mixed loop.

Early Instagram (~10 iOS engineers, rebuilt after AsyncDisplayKit 'warring factions' destroyed each other) offered huge low-hanging fruit: he cut cold start 20% by deferring profile-tab work and crashes 80% with a one-line assertion fix, winning 'the ax,' Instagram's physical weekly impact award.

On the Whiteout redesign he built behind a feature flag with semantic colors instead of a long-lived branch, which saved the launch when a Facebook exec demanded A/B tests the night before shipping; his lesson is that testing is invaluable for mechanics but product direction needs strong opinions — pure experimentation traps you in 1% incrementalism.

Instagram Stories was built in about 2-3 months after he cut the team down to a core of two iOS engineers with no dedicated server engineer: 'if you want to go fast, go small.' The existing friend graph was the unfair advantage over Snapchat, and he invented hold-to-pause because his thumb expected it to work.

His promo strategy: as a mis-leveled IC4 he asked his director for a 4-to-6 double promo knowing HR would refuse, deliberately seeding the idea so it converted into back-to-back promotions (IC5 for Whiteout, IC6 for Stories). Levels and promotions are an incentive system — a dangled carrot — and delays are often pacing, not performance.

IGTV (his IC7 project with Kevin Systrom) failed despite anticipating vertical video: long-form vertical inventory never appeared and creators were horrified by his patented AI landscape-to-vertical reformatting; the era's confusing strategy calls only made sense years later via the antitrust lawsuit's internal memos about Facebook fearing Instagram cannibalization.

IG Labs, his IC8 promo, was a deliberately tiny high-talent-density innovation group for projects that didn't slot into the org chart; it produced collaborative posts and became the talent pool that seeded Threads.

As a rare Tech Lead Director he rejected Facebook's IC/manager separation (preferring Tesla's flexible model, citing Taleb's skin in the game), but paid for it in ratings: 'you're exceeding as an IC but you're meeting as a manager, so you're just meeting.'

On the Thanos-snap hypothetical he says Instagram would likely be better off with half the people: each marginal engineer adds hidden coordination and codebase cost (he waited 4+ hours a day on builds before leaving), and post-Elon Twitter running profitably after ~80% cuts is partial vindication.

Retro, founded with Stories PM Nathan Sharp, is friends-only, ad-free (ads plus algorithmic feeds create misaligned, 'a little bit evil' incentives), capped at 250 friends, and subscription-funded; nearly half of daily users post, and it hit #1 on Taiwan's App Store. Founder tradeoffs: satisfaction and learning up, compensation down.

Talent he admires: Mike Krieger led from the front, coding the Stories neon brush and reviewing diffs at 2 AM with the team; Will Bailey's Stories vision doc proved to be the accurate five-year roadmap and modeled the product-engineer path Facebook culture dismissed; Thomas Dimson, 'the smartest person I've ever met,' built Instagram's ranking and later sold his company to OpenAI.

Advice to new grads: learn the tools of your time — he stood out because iOS was new and seniors hadn't learned it natively; today's equivalent is AI tooling, where new grads can out-skill senior engineers and secure roles despite shrinking junior demand.

nodes.label: Honest Big Tech Layoff Story After 25 Years
nodes.guest_role: Anonymous big tech chief architect laid off after 25 years; ex-consulting group technical director; YouTuber
takeaways: He learned through his network that he was on the layoff list and did a 'switcheroo' to put himself at the top in place of more junior engineers slated to be cut, buying them a few months.

He expects more tech layoffs: companies are under pressure to show returns on heavy AI investment, and cutting headcount keeps revenue flat while dropping costs and earning 'AI efficiency' PR that pumps stock prices.

His layoff package was structured and generous: garden leave, months of severance, PTO payout, and education/equipment stipends; severance is technically negotiable but he wasn't in the mental state to try.

His consulting career path ran junior SWE to senior enterprise architect to group technical director over ~18 years; the senior SWE to senior enterprise architect stretch was his best growth period, driven by strong mentors, while director-level C-suite selling burned him out.

Consulting delivery splits into fly-by-night 'get in, build, get out' projects (often sold just to keep the pipeline full) and multi-year retainers where institutional knowledge enables real quality and maintenance.

Quality holds up despite non-technical clients because of engineering integrity, personal pride in the craft, and firm reputation — deliver a 'steaming pile of turds' once and there is no next customer; it is the consultant's obligation to tell a client their $20M investment is bad.

Hostility toward consultants mostly stems from management consulting, whose headcount-cut recommendations make employees associate arriving consultants with layoffs; technical consulting deals with tools, not people cuts.

Moving from consulting to big tech nearly tripled his compensation via higher base, bonuses, RSUs vesting over 3-4 years, and a ~15% ESPP discount; holding shares through the COVID digital boom multiplied their value. His rule: always take free money (ESPP, 401k match), though with spare cash he'd buy index funds.
After age 40 he found learning new frameworks took weeks instead of days (his Olympic gymnast analogy for aging in tech) and now uses AI tools to polish his code.

The viral layoff vlog began as therapy — easier than journaling — and he is now exploring semi-early retirement, YouTube, and small coding projects after big tech applications went nowhere in a cold job market.

Speaking advice: relax, don't fuse your identity with your subject, and explain like the audience is five — illustrated by a brilliant engineer who couldn't communicate a Drupal memory-cache optimization to non-technical audiences.
Advice to his younger self: work hard, never stop learning, stay flexible — a mentor's lesson that 'if you can stay flexible, you'll survive.'

quotes.speaker: ADE
quotes.quote: What I essentially did was I was able to do a little switcheroo where I put myself to the top of the list and got cut. And that's how I got laid off.

quotes.speaker: ADE
quotes.quote: Companies are looking to decrease headcount, reduce their cost, keep the revenue consistent, and then get some PR points for saying how innovative they are in terms of the AI revolution.

quotes.speaker: ADE
quotes.quote: If it is a steaming pile of turds, it's your obligation to tell your client that, hey, hey, man, like the $20 million that you've invested is a steaming pile of turds.

quotes.speaker: ADE
quotes.quote: I can learn this framework in my 20s in, like, a few days and be effective with it. In my 40s, it's taking me weeks to learn it.

nodes.label: Stanford PhD, AI Researcher and Ex-Citadel Quant Shares His Experience
nodes.guest_role: Stanford PhD, AI researcher at Cartesia, ex-Citadel Securities quantitative researcher
takeaways: A PhD is rarely strictly required outside academia but opens doors in AI research and quant finance and shapes the role: infra/data engineering work doesn't need one, while exploratory architecture-design research benefits; its core skill is that 90% of research is finding the right, tractably-scoped problem.

Research taste comes from constantly skimming the literature (his main feed is a curated X/Twitter follow list grown from Stanford contacts) and maturing gradually from small extensions of existing work to wholly new ideas — skipping that maturation is inadvisable.

Contrary to reputation, he had great work-life balance as a Citadel quant because trader schedules anchored to US market hours shape the whole culture; he believes quant now has better work-life balance than hyper-competitive AI research where people gain edge by outworking rivals.

Quant comp has no standardized levels like tech; it's opaque, driven by firm/team/strategy performance and seniority, heavily bonus-weighted on the alpha side, with paid garden-leave non-competes of typically six months to two years decided by the company at departure.

Finance is far more secretive than tech: pods within one firm may unknowingly work on the same strategies because sharing is forbidden (partly to keep returns uncorrelated); returning to tech he was jarred that people 'just tell you that for free.'

Quant job security is U-shaped: senior quants become expensive relative to market rate and can stop being worth it even when good; the culture is up-or-out with regular trimming, easiest where P&L makes performance unambiguous. Insider-trading workarounds get people fired, sued, and jailed.
His quant firm tier list: Rentech is the mythical gold standard; Jane Street, Citadel, Jump Trading, Hudson River are the strong big names; TGS, XTX, and Radix are elite smaller secretive shops.

He left Citadel for Cartesia — founded by fellow Chris Ré lab members including Mamba creator Albert Gu — deliberately taking startup risk after establishing stability; Cartesia builds voice AI and competes with ElevenLabs (an ~18-month head start) on latency, cost, and voice cloning while pushing toward end-to-end speech models replacing the lossy STT-LLM-TTS pipeline.

Big labs have compute but suffer groupthink and risk-aversion toward out-of-the-box ideas; startups can challenge orthodoxy — e.g., Mamba disproving that transformer scaling had solved sequence modeling, and Cartesia's H-Nets learning tokenization from raw characters.

SSMs compress context into a fixed-size state (like a brain) while transformers' KV cache grows linearly (like a database); hybrids interleaving both are the text cutting edge, but for redundant modalities like audio SSM compression is 'almost a free lunch,' improving quality and inference cost simultaneously.

He's skeptical of research-only startups (all-or-nothing vs resource-rich big labs) and product-only wrapper companies (no moat once base models improve); the durable position is the product-research intersection where the product drives research and model control protects the product.

Advice for SWEs wanting AI research: build fundamentals (coding, math, reading papers) until opportunities come to you; big companies silo you, so a master's credential or a flexible startup helps — Cartesia has converted SWEs to researchers internally, but you need evidence of the skill set.

His biggest regret is the time he spent regretting decisions that didn't matter; advice to his younger self is to focus on deep technical skills and not spread thin — 'a simple recipe that's very hard to follow.'

quotes.speaker: Nimit Sohani
quotes.quote: I would say 90% of the battle in research is actually finding the right problems.

quotes.speaker: Nimit Sohani
quotes.quote: Even the firms that have a reputation for being more open are actually quite secretive versus in tech... It was like, wow, you're just going to tell me that for free.

quotes.speaker: Nimit Sohani
quotes.quote: SSMs are kind of like a brain... whereas transformers are more like a database where you can kind of recall anything in the context.

quotes.speaker: Nimit Sohani
quotes.quote: If your fundamentals are good enough, at some point the opportunities will just come to you rather than the other way around.

nodes.label: Meta Senior Staff (IC7) Eng's Honest Demotion Story
nodes.guest_role: Senior staff engineer (IC7/L7) at Meta, Google, and Cruise; 14.5 years at Google
takeaways: Joining a big company at senior staff means racing a clock: PSC calibrates you against tenured E7s, and with Meta's bottom-10% layoff culture you have about a year to match old-timers; in 14 months Igor self-assessed he had only reached ~E6 performance.
The deeper reason he wanted a demotion: he loves E5/E6 work (coding, debugging, designing, mentoring) while senior staff is a meetings-and-design-docs leadership role that touches much less code.

Meta had no process for dropping a level within the same job category (title conversions like director-to-IC exist, but not E7-to-E6), so his demotion request was refused; instead he took the 'easy way out' via a Google recruiter who had kept pinging him.

His Meta ramp failed where Cruise succeeded because Cruise was smaller with uncrowded scope you could just claim, while Meta's senior IC space felt crowded; Meta also doesn't know how to onboard senior external hires since nearly all its senior people grew up internally.

Onboarding lesson: he spent too much time reading docs instead of building; get your hands dirty and learn in parallel. His final Meta project, estimated at two weeks, ballooned in complexity and even completed would never justify E7 scope.

Meta vs Google culture: Meta sets deliberately aggressive arbitrary deadlines with constant status updates to leadership; deadlines slip and everyone is fine with it, so people learn the pressure is artificial and disengage — 'like elementary school, where the teacher is yelling but kids are still playing.'

Google had no process for boomerang hires at a lower level but made it work; returning as L7 required no interview while returning as L6 nearly required a coding interview (he got an exception), and he rejoined after 3.5 years away with no re-interview, accepting a significant comp drop.

He can be transparent because he's privileged: no visa dependency, spouse's health insurance, kids through college, no mortgage, able to not work for years; he'd advise others not to post about work challenges unless equally secure.

Best quality-of-life level: senior engineer (E5/L5), shielded from upward reporting by TLs and managers; people below senior can't even see what principal-level work is like, so they chase promos blind. To peers urging him to try E7 elsewhere: 'I'm capable of running a marathon, but I don't like running.'
Imposter syndrome insight: comparing yourself to a group of peers is a mistake — each excels at one different thing, and you're measuring yourself against a composite no individual matches.

His Google L7 promo project: porting ads' 10-year-polished CPU-based online ML training infrastructure to first-generation TPUs, starting as a two-person prototype requiring cross-team credibility with TPU/compiler/TensorFlow teams and 18-month-ahead chip orders; testing trick was stripping model computation to simulate an infinitely fast TPU and find bottlenecks; spinning-disk throughput (not storage) became the scarce resource.

Ads under-ordered TPUs conservatively because a mature business must make money — unlike today's LLM players who 'just order more' gigawatts while unprofitable; TPU specialization made sparse embedding lookups a persistent pain.
Mentorship story: an intern he hosted at ~L4 later became the manager of the team he interned on — 'don't underestimate your interns.'

His disillusionment with Google came from the Atlanta office shutdown, where a few hundred people were cut because one senior VP sponsor pulled out — 'at the end of the day, I'm just a cell in the spreadsheet' — years before post-COVID layoffs normalized such cuts.

Advice to younger self: ask whether your company really benefits from what you're building — leadership later killed projects for the same pointlessness he'd sensed; if the answer is no, switch projects (fluid at Google/Meta, near-impossible at Intel and reputedly IBM).

quotes.speaker: Igor
quotes.quote: I'm capable of running a marathon, but I don't like running a marathon. I don't like running. Why would I do that?

quotes.speaker: Igor
quotes.quote: It almost feels like an elementary school, like where the teacher is yelling but kids are still playing... if you constantly put pressure on your people, it just doesn't work anymore.

quotes.speaker: Igor
quotes.quote: You feel like, yeah, at the end of the day, I'm just a cell in the spreadsheet.

quotes.speaker: Igor
quotes.quote: Don't underestimate your interns. They can be really, really good.

nodes.label: Airbnb Staff Eng on How To Not Get Stuck at Senior and Untold Rules of Calibrations
nodes.guest_role: Staff engineer at Stripe, Airbnb, and Instagram; former Airbnb engineering manager; developer productivity specialist
takeaways: Laurent pitched his own transition into management at Airbnb rather than waiting to be asked; he argues most engineers passively hope for what they want instead of explicitly aligning with their manager on career goals.

As a manager he tracked a 'surprise factor' metric: before each performance review, he and the report each wrote the expected rating/promotion on paper and revealed simultaneously; any mismatch (including positive surprises) counted as a coaching failure.

Calibrations have untold, locally-developed rules — e.g. changing roles less than ~2.5 months before cycle end yields an automatic 'meets expectations' — and one such rule cost him a likely staff promotion when he moved into management mid-cycle.

To dispute bureaucratic rules, don't fight them in the calibration room; go one-on-one, gather evidence, and first learn the story behind the rule — sometimes it convinces you, sometimes it exposes a stale precedent.

Instead of measuring engineers by lines of code or PR counts, he instrumented the full journey of a code change at Stripe (think, write, CI, review, merge), turning an unfair individual ranking into actionable org-level friction data.

As uber TL over 35 (eventually 140) engineers at Stripe, he built credibility by embedding in each team for weeks, never justifying decisions with 'it worked at Facebook', and influencing almost entirely by asking questions — he says he never outright rejected a design.

Situational leadership transformed his coaching: match style (directing, selling, supporting, delegating) to the learner's mastery and motivation; a 'failing' Facebook bootcamp mentee became his strongest after he stopped directing and switched to supporting.

The senior-to-staff promotion is a bigger mindset shift than staff-to-senior-staff: staff engineers must independently identify problems, pitch them, and solve them end to end, while seniors typically solve problems others chose; higher levels are the same loop at larger scope.

To find staff-worthy problems, 'channel your inner frustration' — Stripe institutionalized this as friction logs, screenshotting each step of a flow and interrogating every friction point; he could find 50-60 improvements in a single code-change flow.
Airbnb's designer-led culture hired actual firefighters and crisis-management professionals to run incident management — a practice he considered genius and carried to later jobs.
Boredom is a career signal that something must change: it drove his manager-to-IC switch once his team ran itself, and the four-year equity cliff drove his exit from Airbnb back to Meta/Instagram.

Advice to younger self: the industry changes faster than you think — stay on top of it (he reads the weekly top of Hacker News, hn.algolia.com, and has his whole career); most career-impacting book: Radical Candor by Kim Scott.

quotes.speaker: Laurent Charignon
quotes.quote: If you're a good manager, you don't want people to have surprise at performance review time.

quotes.speaker: Laurent Charignon
quotes.quote: As a manager, you represent the interest of the company. You have to put the company first before your report. That's like an untold rule, but that's the way it is.

quotes.speaker: Laurent Charignon
quotes.quote: I managed to do all of my work and all the influence and all the change of direction by asking questions, simply asking questions and getting people to change their mind themselves.

quotes.speaker: Laurent Charignon
quotes.quote: It's much better to say I don't know than to say something wrong. If you claim with confidence something that is wrong, you're going to lose all the respect from your coworkers.

nodes.label: New Grad to Principal Engineer (IC8) at Meta (Career Story)
nodes.guest_role: Principal engineer (IC8) at Meta, original tech lead of Bento
takeaways: His first manager corrected his PhD pretension on day one — 'software engineers have power at this company, don't pigeonhole yourself' — and Adrien has identified as a software engineer rather than a data scientist ever since.

His early rumor-spread research found false rumors spread faster than true ones (more egregious means more viral) and that debunking barely limits spread; the work fed into down-weighting reshares in News Feed.

His IC5 promo came from building Instagram's first A/B testing system as a naive IC4 after leadership told him 'don't bug us' during the AWS migration — he hacked exposure logging through an allow-listed Facebook API endpoint and recruited a PM for air cover, not even knowing promotions existed.
His biggest self-described career mistake was never officially joining early Instagram while embedded there — peers from that ~30-person team reached IC7-IC10 far faster.

Bento started as personal helper scripts to kill data-pipeline boilerplate (300 lines to 20); he negotiated 2-3 months of explicit perf risk with his manager and skip, and it grew into Meta's company-wide notebook platform used by ~60% of data practitioners, earning his IC7/TLM2 promo.

Internal tool adoption strategy: capture newcomers at onboarding (bootcamp/DataCamp) in a fast-growing company so network effects convert the stragglers, and support the legacy systems to build trust before migrating their users.

On perf risk: consequences of underperforming are harsher today than a decade ago, but the playbook is unchanged — make the exploration an explicitly agreed expectation with manager and skip, over-communicate, and be ready to hear no.

Leaving Meta in 2018 to found a startup taught him he wasn't an entrepreneur — founders must delegate the interesting hard problems and scale through others; 'I left the company to do a startup, and I ended up taking a sabbatical instead.'

He has never applied for a job through a website: every career move, including both Meta boomerangs (via former managers James Pearce and Vincent Hardy) and Clubhouse, came through relationships of trust.

The IC8 promo was deliberately engineered: co-writing the packet with a manager who had never done one, socializing with VPs a cycle early, and asking a critical D2 for monthly mentoring six months ahead so there was 'no debate in the room' at calibration.

He carved out the 'TL for experiences' role on a smart-glasses program by taking coordination load (dozens of teams, ~35 PMs) off the existing uber TL with his blessing — scope is taken off someone's plate through over-communication, not stolen; the title came post hoc after a year of doing the work.

Advice to younger self: be a good person, don't be credit-driven, be patient (pushing for IC7 before he was ready cost him cycles), stay curious, and maximize your surface area for luck by taking more shots.

quotes.speaker: Adrien Friggeri
quotes.quote: I often joke that I left the company to do a startup, and I ended up taking a sabbatical instead.

quotes.speaker: Adrien Friggeri
quotes.quote: I don't want to make it sound like getting promoted is just a social game. You need to have the underlying work too... It is also way easier to build the consensus ahead of time so that when you go to a performance calibration session, you don't have a debate in the room.

quotes.speaker: Adrien Friggeri
quotes.quote: Taking something off someone's plate doesn't mean that you're stealing from them — or it is, if you're not talking to them about it.

quotes.speaker: Adrien Friggeri
quotes.quote: I learned very quickly that we're all in this to succeed, and the best way to succeed is to help others succeed.

nodes.label: Anthropic Eng Leader and Ex-Senior Director at Meta on Advice That Changed Her Career
nodes.guest_role: Engineering leader on Claude Code at Anthropic; ex-Senior Director at Meta; 11.5 years at Microsoft
takeaways: War rooms/lockdowns buy focus but tax morale: her Facebook Marketplace growth war room ('we won't exit until we hit the number', 75 experiments launched at once) dragged on 2+ months, and her regret is not being realistic and upfront with the team about how long it would take.

Manager-of-managers advice: treat it as an explicit partnership (divide and conquer by strengths) and build a 'trust but verify' muscle; her nightmare is the 'this is fine' dog meme, so she asks for fast, transparent two-way feedback.

Mentoring and coaching are different modes — mentoring is listening plus advice, coaching is acting as a mirror so the person discovers answers themselves; the best relationships have mentee-set goals and an explicit 'what does success look like in 3/6 months' conversation.
1:1 advice for ICs: push status reporting to async (Slack or a shared living doc) and use the live time for real conversations about learning, career, and digging deeper into the work.

She left Microsoft in 2015 only after shipping TypeScript 1.0, recruited by a former Visual Studio colleague to build what became Facebook Marketplace; the draws were mission (affordable used goods, reuse, small businesses) and escaping the 'safe cocoon' of the Microsoft stack after 11.5 years.
Culture contrast: Facebook Marketplace ran one-week sprints vs four weeks on Visual Studio, and 'nothing at Facebook is somebody else's problem' was the culture she loved.

Dogfooding is her signature leadership practice — using Visual Studio to build Visual Studio, selling household items to seed Marketplace inventory in the Seattle/Hawaii test launch, reproducing VR floor-height bugs at home — framed as a manager's 'maker time' and a trust-builder with engineers and PMs.

Dogfooding tips: integrate it with things that bring you joy (VR workouts, knitting while watching movies in-headset) and hold weekly leadership dogfooding sessions with PM and design partners for fast feedback on nearly-shipped features.

Kindness as an engineering value crystallized during COVID when a report let her cancel a 1:1 for a rare FaceTime slot with her grandmother in assisted living: 'in a world where you can be anything, be kind.'

She wasn't job hunting; using Claude (a Sonnet model) through Meta's internal DevMate tool convinced her AI is 'already here,' and Anthropic's genuine safety-first mission-orientation — evident from day-one onboarding, not just a tagline — pulled her away from a VR role she loved.
Claude Code's iteration loop (idea, build, internal launch, feedback, public launch) feels faster than even early Marketplace days; keeping that agility as the team grows is her top concern.

Career-changing feedback was feedback on receiving feedback: don't 'debug' the giver in the moment — stay in read-only mode, listen, hold questions until the next day, and never make someone justify constructive feedback.

quotes.speaker: Fiona Fung
quotes.quote: That meme of the dog holding the coffee cup in a room on fire, 'This is fine,' is my nightmare.

quotes.speaker: Fiona Fung
quotes.quote: I'm super excited because I'm using Claude Code to build Claude Code. It's the first time I've been able to ship production software in a really long time.

quotes.speaker: Fiona Fung
quotes.quote: When someone cares enough to come to you for constructive feedback, it's already uncomfortable enough. Make sure that that first session, you're just in read-only mode just to learn and listen.

quotes.speaker: Fiona Fung
quotes.quote: Enjoy the ride and these are the good old days, because you'll look back and everything happened so fast.

What you should use 1 on 1s for

nodes.label: Frontline Manager at Meta to Senior Director at Snapchat in 3 years (Career Story)
nodes.guest_role: HeyGen CTO; ex-Senior Director at Snapchat, ex-Director at Square, ex-Meta manager
takeaways: Rong joined Square directly on a director offer — smaller companies will let you perform a level above — and grew the team from ~25 to 50+ engineers in about a year.

The core frontline-manager-to-director gap is that directing is not managing: directors add value through strategy, cross-department resource gathering, and solving the org's hardest problems, not by knowing the most details; he underperformed his first six months until he made this shift.

When hired above people who wanted your role, build trust first (he recommends The Five Dysfunctions of a Team): show reports you are a promoter of their career and unblock things they cannot unblock themselves.

He anchored every career decision to a North Star (become CTO of an AI company), deliberately choosing Snapchat's camera org over the comfortable data org and learning iOS programming from scratch in two months to close product-side gaps.

Facebook's six-week bootcamp — where a VP-level hire sat next to him fixing bugs for six weeks — taught him everyone in engineering must stay technical; as CTO he still writes and reviews code weekly.
He scales himself by picking the top three priorities each week; the best leaders win on time allocation, not hours worked.

Snapchat grew from ~100 employees (he was about the 100th) to ~3,000 in two years and his org to ~250 engineers; every doubling of a team is 'almost like building a new company' and old cultural assumptions must be dropped.
Evan Spiegel gave pixel-level demo feedback reminiscent of Steve Jobs, promised never to fire Snapchat's first 15 engineers, and bought back the original Blue House in 2017 to host board meetings.
Senior-level recruiting is nearly all inbound via personal networks; he has never proactively job-searched and prioritizes working with trusted people who will stay through a company's down cycles.

Don't make promotions the objective: manager growth is heavily constrained by org structure and luck, titles belong to the company not to you, so control impact in your current role and treat everything else as lagging indicators.
At HeyGen he tells candidates upfront there is only a ~1% chance of huge success, deliberately filtering for people who will stay through hard cycles.

quotes.speaker: Rong Yan
quotes.quote: Director is about directing instead of managing. It really opened my mind about the right way to manage managers instead of managing individual contributors.

quotes.speaker: Rong Yan
quotes.quote: The one thing I gradually realized is that levels and titles are things the company gives you; they are not yours.

quotes.speaker: Rong Yan
quotes.quote: Don't make promotions the objective of your work. ... control the things you can control, which is making an impact in your current position. Everything else will be lagging indicators.

quotes.speaker: Rong Yan
quotes.quote: Every single time you double your team, it's almost like you're building a new company.

sections.title: How much of growth is luck?

nodes.label: Boris Cherny (Creator of Claude Code) On How His Career Grew
nodes.guest_role: Creator of Claude Code at Anthropic; ex-Meta/Instagram Principal Engineer (IC8)
takeaways: Coming into Meta under-leveled was lucky: it gave space to build cool things without promo box-checking, and exceeding expectations built compounding reputation; he advises against pushing for level+1 when switching companies.

Side quests drove his growth: Undux became Facebook's most popular state-management framework after he scraped internal support posts, tallied complaints by team, and gave 20-40 per-team tech talks; he also wrote a TypeScript book and ran SF's biggest TypeScript meetup.

The one book he recommends every engineer: Functional Programming in Scala — 'thinking in types' changed how he codes; he got into terse functional languages after a motorcycle accident broke both his arms.

His Staff (IC6) promo came from volunteering Facebook Groups as the guinea pig for the Comet rewrite without asking permission — product teams that help build infra get to shape its abstractions rather than just consume them.

His IC7 promo came from the public-groups project (a 'one-line change' with gnarly data-model, spam, and moderation implications) and from earning senior engineer Bob's trust by disagreeing-and-committing to Bob's migration, then pushing to reverse it when the evidence was in.

Scoping advice: time-box to ~30 minutes, give experts a straw man instead of an open question; for a hard org-wide data-model merge he ran a 3-hour two-team architecture design competition that produced designs 80% identical, creating instant consensus.
On imposter syndrome while directing same-level engineers: levels don't matter at all, and if you never feel imposter syndrome you aren't pushing yourself hard enough.

Automation as free leverage: he tallied every repeated code-review comment in a spreadsheet and wrote a lint rule after a few repeats, eventually automating most of his code reviews; if you hit a problem 2-3 times, others have it too.

He moved to rural Japan for his wife's dream job, joined Instagram Tokyo never having used the app, and turned zero-timezone-overlap into a superpower — no meetings meant far more coding than peers, seeding the Python-to-Hack migration by first befriending its strongest skeptics; he later delegated it to Jake Bolam, following Andy Grove's rule to delegate what you like and know so you can monitor it.
His IC8 promo was a non-event driven by his manager; levels exist for company reasons not engineering reasons, so he optimized for interesting problems and delightful products instead.

Claude Code succeeded because Ben Mann pushed him to 'build for the model six months from now'; it stayed mediocre until Sonnet/Opus 4 landed, after which ~80-90% of Claude Code was written with Claude Code and Anthropic's per-engineer productivity grew ~70% while headcount tripled.

On AI slop: hold model-written code to exactly the same bar as human code; vibe coding only for prototypes and throwaway code, plan-mode pairing as the default, and he still hand-writes the core query loop. Claude Code spread beyond engineers to data scientists and half of Anthropic's sales team.
On competitors like Codex: he doesn't use them and tells his team not to get sidetracked — more competitors signals success; stay focused on users' problems.
Advice to his younger self: just use common sense — big companies systematically pull you away from it.

quotes.speaker: Boris Cherny
quotes.quote: He actually pushed me really hard to be like, don't build for the model of today, build for the model six months from now.

quotes.speaker: Boris Cherny
quotes.quote: At every level, it doesn't matter what level you're at, you should always feel a little bit of imposter syndrome because if you don't, then you're not pushing yourself hard enough.

quotes.speaker: Boris Cherny
quotes.quote: You never delegate the thing you don't want to do. That's the most important rule. You always delegate the thing you do want to do and that you know well because then you can monitor the progress and make sure it's going well.

quotes.speaker: Boris Cherny
quotes.quote: Even though Anthropic has tripled since the start of the year, productivity per engineer has grown almost 70% per engineer because of Claude Code.

nodes.label: Instagram Senior Staff Eng (IC7) On 3 Promos Through Redefining Expectations (Career Story)
nodes.guest_role: Instagram/Threads Senior Staff Engineer (IC7), first engineer on Threads Web
takeaways: Ratings measure delivered impact while promotions (lagging) measure sustained next-level behaviors: Marius greatly exceeded expectations as an IC4 but was blocked by one behaviors miss (too pushy in cross-functional collaboration), then blocked again by Meta's company-wide COVID promo freeze.
His response to blocked promos: keep doing next-level work anyway — you can't be defeatist and regress to your current level's behaviors, because promotions require sustained demonstration.

His IC5 promo came from leading a clearly-classified IC6 project (10+ engineers plus PMs/designers) as an IC4; doing next-next-level work is what separates 'redefines expectations' from 'greatly exceeds', articulated in calibration via 'laddering'.

The opportunity arose from a paternity-leave domino where everyone took n+1 work, but he argues you can increase your 'luck surface area' by positioning yourself as the go-to domain expert (in his case, web platform).

His IC6 promo came from a hard-deadline web project where he flexed into the product-hybrid archetype amid 'fog of war' ambiguity with only part-time PM support; his rule is to cut scope before cutting quality.

There is an IC4, IC5, and IC6 way to solve almost any problem: IC4 adds one React error boundary, IC5 audits and hardens the whole site, IC6 writes the direction-setting note and lint rules so the entire company applies the fix without him — that is leverage.

He deliberately switched to Jake Bolam's IG Web team when web stopped being a priority in his org, aligning his specialty with business priorities, and rewrote Instagram web's aging notifications panel.

On Threads Web he was the sole engineer for the first 6-8 weeks — creating the code-name folder in the monorepo himself — then hired and TL'd the team through launch of logged-out and logged-in clients; the IC7 promo self-review was his easiest ever because attribution was unambiguous.

Ownership means recursive accountability: delegating pieces to senior engineers never absolves the overall DRI; his mentorship model is a driving instructor with a second steering wheel who gradually backs off as trust builds.
He landed nearly 2,000 diffs in the Threads launch year (~10 per working day) while cautioning that diff counts are only directionally interesting and gameable.
IC7+ promotions require a business need — 'an IC7-shaped hole that can only be plugged by an IC7-shaped puzzle piece' — so delivery alone is not enough without the right opportunity.

Every level scared him before he reached it (he once swore he'd never consider IC7); his advice to his younger self, who wrote a blog post every week for years, is 'chill a little bit, it's gonna be fine' — though that public writing is what first got him recruited.

quotes.speaker: Marius Schulz
quotes.quote: You get promoted once you have for a sufficient period of time demonstrated next level behaviors, next level impact.

quotes.speaker: Marius Schulz
quotes.quote: There's a bit of luck involved in getting matched with the right opportunity, but I firmly believe that you can increase your luck surface area by doing the right things.

quotes.speaker: Marius Schulz
quotes.quote: Usually there's an IC4, IC5, and IC6 and maybe even higher way to go about almost any problem.

quotes.speaker: Marius Schulz
quotes.quote: If there's no business need, basically like an IC7 shaped hole that can only be plugged by an IC7 shaped puzzle piece, you're going to have a harder time making a case for a successful IC7 promotion.

Leverage and IC4/5/6 way of solving problems

nodes.label: Robinhood SWE Turned $1B+ Founder on Non-Linear Careers, Being Jaded About Promos, Startup Learnings
nodes.guest_role: Co-founder of Sei Labs, ex-Robinhood SWE
takeaways: A random 'LeetCode and Chill' study night covered the exact interview question (number of islands) Jay later got in his Facebook internship onsite; he credits that lucky break for his entire subsequent career, illustrating that careers move in step functions, not linearly.

Choose employers by what you're optimizing for: max compensation upside requires a very early-stage company that hits big, while learning is fastest at a small company growing quickly; the best controllable heuristic is surrounding yourself with really smart people.
About a third of Jay's 2018 Robinhood new grad class went on to found venture-backed companies, evidence of the caliber of people at fast-scaling startups.

He got jaded nine months into Robinhood after months of 2 AM nights and brutal overnight-batch on-call earned him a 5/5 performance rating with no promotion or compensation change attached; he then deliberately coasted at 'close to average' effort.

Coasting still produced promotions because in a 10x-growth company a competent engineer becomes load-bearing by default; Jay was the only person who knew ~10 different systems and got his first mentee 1.5 years in.

Golden handcuffs kept him 3.5 years: exercising his options would have cost $400,000, so he waited for the July 2021 IPO; his Robinhood equity ended up an order of magnitude more than an equivalent big tech stint would have paid.

Inside account of GameStop: on Jan 28, 2021 Robinhood turned off buys because T+2 settlement required billions in collateral overnight, not to manipulate prices, and Jay believes the company miscommunicated this 'potentially on purpose' while fundraising billions under regulatory hostility.

Engineers over-index on the risk of quitting: competent people always find work again, and a failed founding attempt makes you more valuable to early-stage startups (though not big companies) because you learn product validation, marketing, BD, and go-to-market.

Fundraising lesson from raising $35M three weeks after the Terra collapse: land a tier-one lead investor (Multicoin led their $5M seed) and everything else, including follow-on funding and BD, becomes dramatically easier as other funds herd behind the stamp.

Founding requires little engineering skill ('a competent L4 can do perfectly fine') since most businesses face mundane problems; his advice to his younger self is to be less scared, think decisions through, then pull the trigger quickly, and he wishes he'd left Robinhood earlier.

quotes.speaker: Jayendra Jog
quotes.quote: There was no promotion, no compensation change tied to that, and I was just like, why did I do this?

quotes.speaker: Jayendra Jog
quotes.quote: Robinhood did not turn off the buys because they were trying to manipulate the price. They turned off the buys because they needed to put up more collateral, and I think they miscommunicated this potentially on purpose.

quotes.speaker: Jayendra Jog
quotes.quote: If you get a tier one lead investor, everything else is super easy after that.

quotes.speaker: Jayendra Jog
quotes.quote: You should think through any decision you make, but then afterwards, pull the trigger quickly.

nodes.label: CloudKitchens CTO on Intelligence, Regrets, Steve Jobs and Travis Kalanick Stories
nodes.guest_role: CTO of CloudKitchens, ex-Uber Senior Staff, ex-Google, ex-Apple intern
takeaways: Brian reached Senior Staff at Uber by 25 through 90-100 hour weeks: he and a peer prototyped the options their team debated each day after hours, becoming de facto tech leads within months and top-3 committers across Uber mobile; he claims career growth vs hours worked is superlinear because strategic work only goes to the already-proven.

The cost was real: he once collapsed on a street walking home from Uber, unable to move for 15 minutes; his burnout early-warning signal now is becoming grumpy and impatient, usually flagged by his partner.
Luck dominates infrequent decisions like choosing a company every few years, but not high-frequency daily actions; picking a meritocratic culture is the biggest controllable-yet-lucky variable.

He argues IQ correlating with job performance is one of the most well-researched results in applied psychology; good interviews force on-the-spot reasoning (e.g., read a 5-page postmortem in 5 minutes and debate restructuring the team), LeetCode is just a gameable IQ proxy, and he is seriously considering literal IQ screens, starting with PM interviews.

His prioritization formula: expected value of the work times probability of success with-vs-without you, divided by your time cost; this often means avoiding the company's well-staffed top priority in favor of being the hero on priority #3, and juniors should mine planning spreadsheets' 'why we care' columns to build expected-value intuition.

He procrastinated on management so badly that a report asked after three months whether Brian was actually his boss; after committing, his org doubled every six months, driven by assuming every inherited org has an order-of-magnitude improvement and truth-seeking with the team (e.g., killing low-value integrations among 1,200 cut pages and improved the product).

CloudKitchens rebuilt its leveling ladder after Travis Kalanick rejected an Uber-style draft as lazily over-indexed on scope: ~1,000 real performance examples from books and calibration notes were distilled to 250 attributes and PCA-collapsed into 12 skill gradients rewarding truth-seeking (being right when questioning foundational decisions) rather than position-oriented, politics-rewarding competencies like influencing other teams.

On big tech honesty: Amazon-style companies underpay top performers 'because they can get away with it'; at Google a non-performer was protected for being likable, and several managers told him of an unwritten policy of retaining idle smart people so competitors couldn't hire them; standards fall entropically until a company dies or a trauma (like the DeepMind existential push) resets them.

CloudKitchens' thesis: in a few decades no one should cook their own food; they have robots cutting restaurant labor costs 50%, logistics cutting meal costs 30-40%, real estate halving cost per meal, and Otter processing 18% of the world's orders, chasing trillions in the $2.5T/year US food market while staying stealth to avoid press distraction and copycat competitors.

As an Apple intern he accidentally killed his own sunlight-readability project by downplaying it in front of Steve Jobs, who rolled his eyes and walked away; he doesn't regret failures he couldn't avoid, only moments he knew the truth mid-project and stayed silent. Advice to his younger self: be more courageous, less embarrassed, and possibly go to therapy.

quotes.speaker: Brian Attwell
quotes.quote: But if you want to be senior staff at one of the world's best tech companies by the time you're 25, this is the only way to do it.

quotes.speaker: Brian Attwell
quotes.quote: An interview that anyone can pass regardless of IQ if they can practice enough does not build a strong engineering team.

quotes.speaker: Travis Kalanick (via Brian Attwell)
quotes.quote: Guys, the core problem here is that this is lazy. You are over-indexing on scope. It's uninsightful, and you could do so much better.

quotes.speaker: Brian Attwell
quotes.quote: If you have entropy in a system, standards will naturally fall over time until the company dies.

nodes.label: Uber Distinguished Eng On Unfair Promos, Influence, Engineering Regrets (Career Story)
nodes.guest_role: Former Distinguished Engineer at Uber, now AI startup engineer
takeaways: Joakim's Distinguished-level project (Odin) began as his Denmark team running MySQL shards in Docker with declarative intent-based management when Kubernetes was barely in alpha, and grew into the platform for all of Uber's stateful workloads: ~120,000 physical servers and ~500,000 databases operated by a team of about 20.

Platform adoption strategy: build it and prove it on your own team first, expand through friendly orgs to skeptics rather than forcing it; but a final top-down mandate ('you will not be allowed to have your own service any longer') was still necessary, because full alignment never happens bottom-up alone.

His model of promotions: level tracks how many people are affected by your work, so a project with natural scope expansion drags promotions along automatically for you and the whole team; his Senior-to-Principal-to-Distinguished steps each followed a stage of Odin's growth, helped by the luck of one long-running project.

Promo committees are structurally unfair: his first one reviewed hundreds of engineers in a single day with no prep or materials, decided by how well each manager pitched; even structured processes can't handle dependencies that ghost you, canceled projects, or isolated teams who were never told what good looks like.

His core promo advice: software engineers must write code at every level, ideally every day; craftsmanship is learned by watching others; 'running code beats perfect code any day'; and instead of hunting promotion-worthy scope, focus on providing value and the promotion will follow.

Contrarian delegation take: staying hands-on preserves system knowledge and trust; delegate the hard interesting work down to people who can barely manage it so they grow fast, and keep the boring 'shoveling shit' work yourself since you have nothing left to prove.

Engineers don't trust management because authority without demonstrated understanding earns polite nods and silent non-compliance; his best influence outcome is overhearing someone champion, as their own conviction, an idea they once rejected from him.

Mentorship: pick mentees outside your own org to learn about other parts of the company and plant seeds of culture change in distant teams; keep mentoring informal with no programs or schedules, and don't disqualify mentees for asking the wrong questions.

Senior-level politics was the scary discovery that 'nobody's in control, there's an illusion of control'; he coped by saying no a lot, avoiding dead-end projects, and using the Denmark timezone as a shield that limited exposure to SF meetings to a couple of evening hours.

He left Uber when new management imposed top-down decisions engineers couldn't stand behind, notably a cloud migration justified on cost that no engineer believed and that only worked after a 50% cost cut that would have been cheaper on-prem; refusing to 'stick around and take the money,' he joined an AI enterprise automation startup rather than follow ex-colleagues to Databricks.

Biggest Uber engineering mistakes: chronic inability to effect global change (a tracing mandate re-emailed for ~3 years never got done) and a software networking stack built in Node that took years to untangle; the early 'let builders build' freedom became deadly at scale and Uber never handled the freedom-to-structure transition well.

Advice to his younger self after feeling imposter syndrome joining from small tech: just try stuff, the big-tech people were not much better after all, 'it's not rocket science, it is just computers'; let curiosity lead and keep re-checking whether there's something better to work on.

quotes.speaker: Joakim Recht
quotes.quote: The more people who are affected by what you do well, the higher you get in the level... a number of promotions will happen automatically. And not just for you, but the entire team.

quotes.speaker: Joakim Recht
quotes.quote: A software engineer needs to write code. If you're not writing code, you're not a software engineer.

quotes.speaker: Joakim Recht
quotes.quote: Running code beats perfect code any day.

quotes.speaker: Joakim Recht
quotes.quote: Then you realize at some point nobody's in control. There's an illusion of control, but there's just people randomly getting ideas and pushing them out.

nodes.label: Shopify Distinguished Eng (L10) on Principal+ Engineering, Career Story, Regrets | Ilya Grigorik
nodes.guest_role: Distinguished Engineer (L10) at Shopify, ex-Google Director, PostRank founder
takeaways: Waterloo's co-op rotation gives students about six stigma-free internships to test industries and company sizes before committing, which Ilya calls the school's underappreciated superpower.

Ilya deferred and never finished grad school to found PostRank, a 'better PageRank' ranking posts by social engagement signals, after realizing live investor conversations taught more than entrepreneurship coursework.

Google acquired PostRank during its race against Facebook on social; it was close to an acquihire — Google wanted the team and experience, and the product was rebuilt from scratch inside Google Analytics at exabyte scale.

Even in an acquisition, the whole PostRank team including founder/CTO Ilya went through Google's full interview panel to determine leveling; team, IP, and customers are negotiated as separate threads.

Though told he was on track for director in Google Analytics, Ilya took a lateral, likely down-leveled IC role in the 'Make the Web Fast' group to gain self-direction and work on a domain he loved — the right call in retrospect.

He treats IC vs. manager as 'tours of duty' chosen per problem: his W3C/IETF browser performance metrics work created an adoption problem that couldn't scale with one person, so he became Director of Developer Relations to compress 3-5 years of ecosystem adoption into 1-2.
His success test as a principal engineer is disappearing 'Homer Simpson into the bushes' — if the team keeps executing well without him, he did his job of upleveling them.

Anyone who asks for a principal engineer checklist isn't ready for the level: you get parachuted into ambiguous terrain and must map problems, build alliances with directors/VPs, and find where your skillset applies within about a week.

Distinguished Engineer is a VP-equivalent role defined by dynamic range — technically from bare metal to business requirements, and in execution style from fog-of-war rapid iteration to slow, careful platform API design with years-long consequences.

'Don't be the best, be the only': build a portfolio of 80-90% competencies (design, sales, marketing, writing, management) whose intersection makes you uniquely capable and antifragile — 'literally probability math' — and LLMs now make acquiring the first 80% faster than ever.
Career regret: don't let the pressure of past success filter what you publish or try — early embarrassing writing and code were the feedback loop that drove his growth.

quotes.speaker: Ilya Grigorik
quotes.quote: I have this belief that a better way, a more resilient way to build your career is to optimize for being the only person as opposed to the best person.

quotes.speaker: Ilya Grigorik
quotes.quote: People that have to ask that question by definition are not principal engineers, right? Because the problems become ambiguous enough at that level where you kind of have to figure it out yourself.

quotes.speaker: Ilya Grigorik
quotes.quote: Don't let the pressure of your success stop you from trying.

nodes.label: Mozilla Firefox CTO on Browser War Stories and the Path to Distinguished Engineer
nodes.guest_role: CTO of Mozilla Firefox, former Distinguished Engineer
takeaways: Early Mozilla had no onboarding guardrails ('get on IRC and Bugzilla and figure things out'), which selected for self-motivated engineers; code ownership worked by 'who owns this?' — 'you do now'.

Google was originally Firefox's biggest ally — the default-search deal made Mozilla financially sustainable — but after Chrome launched in 2008 the relationship decayed through organizational incentives, the 'year of a hundred oopses' of always-unintentional Download Chrome banners on google.com.

Firefox genuinely fell behind Chrome on multi-process architecture, plugin sandboxing, and JavaScript speed, while Mozilla's failed Firefox OS mobile bet drained resources exactly when the browser needed them most.

Bobby self-initiated 'Slaughterhouse', a year-plus effort to architecturally eliminate a class of JS/DOM-binding exploits; when Mozilla's Bugzilla was compromised by a threat actor, all but about 2 of roughly 45 exfiltrated security bugs were already fixed — proof you must get ahead of security before you lose the luxury of time.

He once accused a friendly security researcher of selling Mozilla out after recognizing his exploit-writing style in an in-the-wild zero-day; the researcher went off the grid and resurfaced via a bowling-alley phone call weeks later — the actual breach was Mozilla's own bug tracker.

Rust was Mozilla's answer to C++ memory-safety exploits and single-threaded engines on multi-core hardware; a dozen Servo engineers couldn't out-build hundreds of Chromium engineers, so Mozilla uplifted Servo's parallel CSS engine into Firefox Quantum (led by Bobby), improving Amazon.com rendering ~25%.
Quantum's launch made him one of fewer than five principal engineers at Mozilla; his manager then made him spend one day a week without opening a terminal to think about broader impact.

On promos: focus on impact, not titles — promo-hacking and box-checking is 'extremely annoying' to leadership, and 'you don't want the people in leadership to find you to be annoying'; frame updates as visibility and team credit, not self-promotion.

The CTO told him new Distinguished Engineers would be named for the first time in nearly a decade — and he wouldn't be one, because the bar had shifted from codebase mastery to industry-wide impact; he found it inspiring, like discovering an untrained muscle, and later made DE under a hybrid of both definitions.

Mozilla's DE archetypes are world-changing IC impact: Martin Thomson (HTTP/2, QUIC, WebRTC standards), Luke Wagner (asm.js to WebAssembly, forging cross-browser consensus), Timothy Terriberry (Opus/Daala to royalty-free AV1 video).

His top advice for aspiring DEs is to write: writing is the process of thinking, and you should not have an LLM write for you because you lose the thinking that drives growth; documents should be no longer than what you have to say.

On the AI browser wars: most 'AI browsers' are front ends on Chromium; Firefox is the last independent engine, which is a business cost center but buys a seat at the standards table. AI in consumer tech is 'water running downhill'; the risks are the web becoming a backend detail for agents and Chrome's unspecified proprietary browser-LLM APIs entrenching Gemini.

quotes.speaker: Bobby Holley
quotes.quote: He referred to it as the year of a hundred oopses, where every time Google would do something that would hurt Firefox, it would always be framed as unintentional.

quotes.speaker: Bobby Holley
quotes.quote: Think about your ambition in terms of the impact that you want to have as opposed to the status you want to achieve.

quotes.speaker: Bobby Holley
quotes.quote: The process of writing is the process of thinking... do not use an LLM to produce text for you... you are losing the opportunity to do the thinking yourself.

quotes.speaker: Bobby Holley
quotes.quote: AI is coming to consumer technology like water running downhill, and this is not something that is going to be stopped or reversed.

nodes.label: Intern to Microsoft Distinguished Engineer in 11 Promotions (Career Story)
nodes.guest_role: Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft; co-creator of NuGet and SignalR, .NET Core architect
takeaways: Microsoft levels run 59-70+ with two levels per title band and three principal levels; you can't tell someone's exact level from their title, and Distinguished Engineer (70) is the final partner level, below only Technical Fellow.

He differentiated at the college career fair by demoing a self-built game on a laptop, landing a Microsoft internship in 2006 when interviews were puzzles (Die Hard 3 water jugs, counting gas stations in Florida) rather than LeetCode.

His early-career engine was 'code is currency': troll forums for customer problems, build the prototype first, then show PMs and architects — first promotion came 8 months in, and a director told people 'David has energy, go talk to him'.

SignalR began as his own nights-and-weekends project (inspired by Google Docs co-editing, pre-WebSockets) that Microsoft later made official with a team of ~8 — proof that 'you can just do things'; a good track record then earns you more leeway to fail and try things.

He deliberately attached himself to an architect whose career he wanted (David Ebbo), coding his ideas and mimicking his behaviors until he became one himself, and later requested a Technical Fellow mentor (Jeffrey Snover, PowerShell inventor) once he decided he wanted DE.

The senior-to-principal leap is doing more through others: as senior-level co-architect of .NET Core with ~30 engineers, trying to co-review every change burned him out within a year, forcing him to learn delegation, trust, and letting people fail.

Distinguished promotion is peer-reviewed by technical fellows against a decade-long body of work; his package leaned on ~7 years of deferred .NET Core impact. Guido van Rossum shares his level, and Technical Fellows (Anders Hejlsberg, Dave Cutler) require industry-wide impact — 'everyone's a unicorn'.

Best mentor advice he got: go directly to the promotion deciders and ask where you stand, and amplify strengths to superhuman rather than fixing weaknesses — 'no one gets promoted to these roles for being average'.

To escape the partner-level meeting trap, he blocks a minimum of 8 hours a week to think and build ('decline, decline, decline') and still sends pull requests daily, refusing to be an architect who doesn't know the code.

Interviews test writing new code but the higher-leverage skill is debugging — he'd hand candidates a crash dump; universities should teach making surgical fixes in huge existing codebases, since CS is not software engineering.
Big-company tips: before changing roles ask when the last reorg was (reorgs reveal where the org is heading), and evaluate a job move by what you'd hate, not what you'd love.

He turned down better pre-IPO Twitter and GitHub offers to stay (accumulated network, autonomy, a new project every two years), was depressed for a week when Twitter IPO'd, yet would tell a mentee today to take the hot outside offer; 10 years on .NET Core taught him every punted small issue eventually becomes a big one.

The Ballmer-to-Satya culture change was from competing teams and shouting matches to incentivized collaboration — reviews dinged you for cloning another team's work and leaders modeled pulling quiet people into conversations.

Regrets and advice: early arrogance ('being right means nothing' without patience and empathy), passing on a friend's 2008 Bitcoin venture, and — for his younger self — negotiate your salary and get multiple offers.

quotes.speaker: David Fowler
quotes.quote: I'm a code-first, ask questions later kind of person. My whole thing is code is currency. I can show you a working sample. It's not PowerPoint, it's not a document.

quotes.speaker: David Fowler
quotes.quote: The funny thing is you get a chance to be more permissionless the better your track record is. The more success you have, the more leeway you get to fail, which gives you space to try more things.

quotes.speaker: David Fowler
quotes.quote: You could work on the things you're not good at and be average at those, or you can work on things that you're really good at and be superhuman at those things.

quotes.speaker: David Fowler
quotes.quote: My GitHub is a graveyard of projects... Everything you build will teach you a new skill that you don't even know you have until later on.

sections.title: Career regrets and work life balance

nodes.label: Stripe CTO on What Grew His Career, Hiring Without Leetcode, Coding as a Leader (Career Story)
nodes.guest_role: Former Stripe CTO (7 years), ex-Google VP of Engineering, founder of /dev/agents
takeaways: Singleton's career engine at Google was agency: as a junior engineer he removed the google.com-to-google.com/m mobile redirect that nobody wanted to touch, saving ~70 years of user time per year, and he stayed nearly 12 years only because every 18-24 months he realized he was still learning.

Being a manager and being an IC are different jobs: if you try management, do it full time rather than alongside IC work, ideally somewhere that allows a trial-and-return; he first tries to talk people out of management, which only fits if you draw energy from getting more out of others.

Even the most senior leaders should experience the team's work at the 'one foot level': his quarterly 'engineer-acation' meant ~3 days embedded on a team shipping a small real project plus a detailed friction log, which fed his developer-productivity strategy as CTO.

His biggest managing-managers lesson: perfectly happy teams are rarely performing at their best potential; he won Google's team-voted Great Manager Award and later concluded he didn't deserve it, aiming instead for 'type two fun' and insisting low performers be managed.

The VP-vs-senior-EM jump was about leading functions beyond engineering (design, product, partnerships) and losing the confidence of being able to do every job in the org; other functions don't exist to 'feed the engineers'.

Communication: speaking improves only with deliberate reps (his manager booked him 1000-person keynotes); senior leaders are 'always on stage'; keep dynamic range so people believe you; circulate drafts of important writing to reviewers, a habit learned at Stripe.

Scaling as a senior leader means designing org systems like an operating system: Stripe's nested weekly ops reviews were modeled directly on Amazon's (Charlie Bell walked him through it); a process is only legitimate while clearly net positive for those inside it.

Far-timezone sites need complete missions and autonomy; splitting one team across Mountain View and London is the worst setup; site leads should spend time at HQ carrying context home; sites should have a clan identity rooted in company culture.

He joined Stripe by asking for advice on a startup idea ('anytime you want money, ask for advice; anytime you want advice, ask for money') and Patrick Collison converted it into recruiting; senior hires almost always begin as advice conversations with people happy elsewhere.

Culture must be invested in deliberately from day one: Stripe's operating principles (users first, meticulous in your craft) are concrete and distilled from the most effective employees' behaviors, enforced through exemplifying them and through systems like hiring and blameless incident review.

quotes.speaker: David Singleton
quotes.quote: That ultimately ended up saving something like 70 years of user time every year... You get a real bug for those things, like being able to just follow your nose to learn and take agency to do stuff that matters.

quotes.speaker: David Singleton
quotes.quote: What I learned later, especially as I started managing managers, was that those teams that are perfectly happy are rarely performing at their very best potential.

quotes.speaker: David Singleton
quotes.quote: When you're a leader, you have to continually step onto the balcony. You're on the dance floor doing stuff in the org, but you have to prompt yourself to step onto the balcony and look down at how everything is working.

quotes.speaker: David Singleton
quotes.quote: Someone once told me, anytime you want money, ask for advice, and anytime you want advice, ask for money.

nodes.label: Behind the Scenes of this Podcast So Far
nodes.guest_role: Host of The Peterman Post; Meta engineer and author of The Developing Dev newsletter (interviewed by Brandon)
takeaways: Ryan started the podcast as deliberate practice for speaking: publishing forces him to pore over every word in editing, a far stronger feedback loop than Toastmasters, and he set a deliberately lax goal of one episode per month.

The format is inspired by internal Facebook career-story posts written by very senior ICs, 50-minute reads Ryan devoured early in his career, hence having super-senior engineers tell their career stories on the show.
The Philip Su episode was the first breakout: 'the graphs were all going vertical' within a day or two, which Ryan read as product-market fit for hidden senior-IC career stories.

The Ethan Evans episode confirmed the pattern; Ryan calls Evans one of the best speakers he's seen in tech, and its aggressive trailer worked so well he plans tasteful trailers on YouTube but minimal or no trailers on Spotify/Apple for recurring listeners.

His dream guest list targets hidden super-senior engineers famous only inside their orgs; since LinkedIn and Google can't surface them, he sources through his big-tech network and by asking the audience which senior IC in their org impresses them, which has produced upcoming Apple/Google/Microsoft guests.
The hardest podcasting-specific habit change: suppressing verbal backchannel (uh-huh, right, yeah) while guests speak and nodding vigorously instead, because acknowledgment noises ruin the audio.

Balancing content with a full-time Meta job is brutal: for about two years he stayed up until 4:00 AM every Thursday writing the newsletter, and Gergely Orosz's warning about 'burning the candle at both ends' proved right.

quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman
quotes.quote: I pour over every word in the editing because I know that people are going to listen to it. That is a very powerful commitment mechanism and a very powerful feedback loop.

quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman
quotes.quote: After I posted it, just in a day or two, the graphs were all going vertical... This can be kind of product market fit for the podcast.

quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman
quotes.quote: I want to find these hidden super senior engineers who no one knows at all... But you've probably never heard of them.

quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman
quotes.quote: For me, every Thursday for about two years, I was staying up till 4:00 AM writing a newsletter.
