nodes.label: Meta Senior Manager (M2) on Manager Career Growth, PIPs, Amazon vs Meta | Stefan Mai
nodes.guest_role: Former Meta Senior Manager (M2), ex-Amazon SDM, co-founder of Hello Interview
takeaways: Mai's first week at Amazon (2012, early self-service advertising) began with his manager committing the team to a 6-8 month nights-and-weekends death march; 50-60 hour weeks were fairly common for ambitious big-tech engineers.

His manager transition came prematurely and took ~18 months of failing; the core early mistake was not knowing how to get reports to reveal their real goals, fixed via active listening instead of transactional API-style one-on-ones.

Storytelling is a bedrock manager skill: narrate the team's trajectory and define winning, because a team that feels it's winning avoids zero-sum politics, which fester exactly when winning slows down.

Manager-of-managers is a different job with real 'mutiny' risk: in flat orgs, leaf managers advance by displacing those above them via skip-level griping; and two teams given overlapping charters will undercut each other rather than run a clean experiment.

Senior leaders have fewer, more binary levers: you cannot do an underperforming manager's job for them, and at the top (e.g. Zuckerberg with an SVP) underperformance simply means being let go, not coached.
Managers are not layoff-proof: companies target manager-to-IC ratios and span-of-control reductions put managers first on the chopping block.

Amazon and Meta performance systems converge: public ratings plus a private VP/director process (Amazon 'top tier', Meta discretionary equity) picks linchpins by trajectory and irreplaceability; Meta pays its top quintile dramatically more (some cleared 2x peers) while frugal Amazon pulls that back.

The performance process costs roughly $15,000 per engineer per cycle by his own estimate, but 'the integrity of the performance review process is the whole ball game' - if people stop believing it rewards performance, they stop performing.

Low-performer quotas are universal at scale: roughly 7-15% (up to 20%) get pushed into the bottom bucket ('needs improvement' at Amazon, 'meets some/meets most' at Meta), where PIPs engage; the nightmare is a director forcing a manager to retroactively downgrade someone they praised.

PIPs are first a legal paper trail, but people genuinely escape them because the trail loses legal credibility if nobody survives; Amazon HR was savage box-checking while Meta HR exhausted every avenue to protect its employer reputation.

AI cheating has made remote interviews 'horrible' and focus/copy-paste detection only catches the unsophisticated; he expects assessments to evolve (low-level design, AI-allowed assignments) rather than anti-cheat to win, and notes cheaters get destroyed by ruthless performance management once hired.

For OpenAI/Anthropic interviews: OpenAI notoriously reuses question areas, and behavioral rounds reward raw technical achievement - the staff-engineer 'seven docs and six aligned teams' story lands poorly there.

Job hopping helps ambitious juniors but is a red flag at senior levels (staff+ moving every 18-24 months gets second-guessed); conversely, deep tenure's credibility snowball can become a shackle if you're forever known for one system.

Advice to younger self: find mentors instead of going alone, and write explicit plans with high agency - walking into his director's office with a list of things he didn't yet know how to do was the moment his director knew he'd succeed.

quotes.speaker: Stefan Mai
quotes.quote: I've got an idea. This is what we're gonna build, and we're gonna work nights and weekends until it happens.

quotes.speaker: Stefan Mai
quotes.quote: If you assume that leading other managers is going to be leading engineers, then you are going to fail very quickly. For a lot of people, it'll just be mutiny.

quotes.speaker: Stefan Mai
quotes.quote: The integrity of the performance review process is the whole ball game. If you get that wrong and people don't believe the process is gonna produce outcomes for them, they're not going to perform.

quotes.speaker: Stefan Mai
quotes.quote: I think PIPs are first and foremost a legal paper trail, but in order to maintain credibility of that legal paper trail, people have to make it through it.

nodes.label: Instagram Staff (IC6) Promo Despite 10 Team Switches in 9 Years (Career Story)
nodes.guest_role: Ex-Meta/Instagram Staff Engineer (IC6), prototyper on Threads and AI teams
takeaways: Sash switched teams 10 times in 9 years at Meta, choosing teams for 'pure fun' and zero-to-one ambiguity rather than impact, and still grew from IC4 to Staff (IC6) as a byproduct.
Being comfortable in ambiguous, poorly defined spaces is a differentiating asset in big tech, where many strong engineers need clear specs before they can execute.

On an experimental hardware team he landed only ~2 diffs in 6 months; the team was explicitly reviewed on de-risking directions, showing how big tech protects risk-takers from performance-review downside without guaranteeing promos.

He was recruited to Threads pre-launch because of his prototyping reputation, and responded by immediately building a working Instagram-to-Mastodon (ActivityPub) bridge and a stripped-down text-first Instagram iOS app over Christmas break.

Threads went from first prototype to public launch in about 5-6 months with a small team built on Instagram's primitives, timed against Musk's Twitter acquisition, and became Meta's poster child for lean product building.
The Instagram blockchain/NFT team taught him that products usually fail on misaligned business incentives rather than technology; the project died with crypto winter.

Prototypes are the highest-fidelity communication medium: his GPT-3-era RAG demo over Facebook's help FAQ drew about 50 cold reach-outs from across the company, building visibility that documents can't.
Goodhart's law makes any chosen metric degrade as people game it, and much of what matters isn't measurable, but large companies still need frameworks because nothing else scales.
His advice to his younger self: don't obsess over fixing weaknesses; keep them out of your way and position yourself where your strengths shine.
He left Meta for startups because it's a two-way door: financially irrational but a real test of what your ideas are worth, and big tech will hire you back.

quotes.quote: You can have 500 decks or you can have one prototype that works and people can play with and it just creates excitement and all of a sudden people jump on the project.

quotes.speaker: Sash Zats
quotes.quote: Peter is a copilot, an incredible copilot... But he doesn't hallucinate and that's important!

quotes.speaker: Sash Zats
quotes.quote: I'm just not thinking about impact too well, and that allows me to pick the teams that are completely irrational and high risk and God knows if there are even rewards in there.

quotes.speaker: Sash Zats
quotes.quote: You want to position yourself where you can showcase your strength and where your weaknesses are not getting in your way.

nodes.label: Amazon VP On Promotions, Getting Fired Twice, Working With Bezos | Ethan Evans
nodes.guest_role: Retired Amazon VP (Prime Video, App Store, Twitch, Prime Gaming), executive coach
takeaways: Evans was let go twice around the .com bust for abrasiveness (the second time as a 'layoff of one'), and an interviewer's remark that truly valuable people get kept triggered him to deliberately rebuild his soft skills around questions and listening.
Be 'strategically annoying' rather than abrasive: push for your opinions, but if people stop disagreeing to your face they organize behind your back, and without allies you dig your own grave.

His director promotion came from an outsized event: he told his SVP 'unless you order me not to do this, I'm going to do it,' bet on the TiVo partnership, and the promo doc read 'without Ethan, we wouldn't have had TiVo.'

He pushed for promotion with unassailable wording ('my career is very important to me, and I need to know if it's as important to Amazon'), and admits leaders often promote the polite agitator over the patient team player because retention triage is real.
His 'magic loop' VP promotion took 2.5-3 years: an explicit partnership where he did everything his manager needed in exchange for the manager staking his credibility on the promotion.
He once cost a star SDE1 a promotion by not knowing Amazon's twice-yearly promo cycle; the report bears the risk, so reports should verify their manager actually knows the process and its deadlines.

As Twitch integration liaison he gave up an 800-person org to influence CEO Emmett Shear with zero authority; Twitch's gaps were indifference to profitability while Jassy demanded it, and Shear having to hire over loyal early employees.

Bezos as founder could gamble the company (telling his CFO 'how much is that? because I might want to spend it') and felt emotionally supportive; Jassy was a classic hired business leader whose probing style felt like waiting for Evans to fail.

VP reviews reward hitting interim goals rather than the vision: he was rated well every year while Prime Gaming never approached the Tencent-scale vision, and zombie products persist because shutting them down admits failure.

Amazon's unregretted attrition goal (~4-7%/year) came back after an experiment showed managers stop having hard conversations without it; PIPs are self-fulfilling and everyone he coached through one was fired anyway, so use the PIP time to job hunt.
A manager can fire any single person they choose: they strike first with the narrative (700 pull requests can be spun as upleveling the team or as nitpicking), and HR will not rescue you.
Advice to his younger self: always prefer high-growth companies (Amazon grew ~100x under him, an 'escalator' he also climbed) and build reputation and relationships early so companies call you.

quotes.speaker: Ethan Evans
quotes.quote: People say I'm a loose cannon, but that's only 'cause I'm pointed at them.

quotes.speaker: Ethan Evans
quotes.quote: My career is very important to me, and I need to know if it's as important to Amazon, because if it's not as important to Amazon as it is to me, I have to think about that.

quotes.speaker: Ethan Evans
quotes.quote: And he said, Tom, how much is that, because I might wanna spend it.

quotes.speaker: Ethan Evans
quotes.quote: So what you need to do is spend that time on the PIP looking for your next job, because you're going to be fired.

quotes.speaker: Ethan Evans
quotes.quote: You're bringing a knife to a gunfight. They're the one with the gun, and you're probably gonna lose.

nodes.label: Tech Lead for Meta's Most-Used Programming Language (Promotion Story)
nodes.guest_role: Meta Senior Staff Engineer (IC7), tech lead for the Hack programming language
takeaways: Dwayne interviewed at pre-IPO Facebook only as practice for Google but was won over by real engineering problems (memcache scaling, custom PHP runtime) and care signals like an unsolicited offer increase and CTO Bret Taylor taking MIT new grads to lunch.
MIT's career value was opportunity access and name recognition rather than the education itself; equally capable colleagues without degrees reached Facebook only by chance.

His breakout project, typing an untyped core PHP API with Hack, was scoped at 1-2 weeks but took a quarter, and about 20% of migrated call sites revealed real bugs, which made Meta take Hack seriously.
Static typing wins at industry scale because large codebases are an information-communication problem: types communicate intent, power tooling, and provably eliminate classes of errors.

His 'uncanny valley of type systems' concept: as a codebase nears full static typing, remaining unreliable types feel increasingly off-putting, so things get worse just before 100%; it became the Hack team's rallying vision and justified removing unsound PHP behaviors.

IC5 promotion: the half where he wrote the least code of his career (raising Hack coverage from ~20-30% to ~60-70% by influencing others) earned his highest rating ever, teaching him the job is identifying and solving problems, not code output.

IC6 promotion: his manager's rocket-ship analogy ('I only cared about you getting us out of orbit') forced him to hand off his year-long collections redesign once aligned, and the handoff itself demonstrated next-level scope; both promos surprised him because his model of the next level was wrong.

He became a TLM reluctantly, scaled to ~13 reports including managers, but coached ICs well while giving org design 'vibe management'; he returned to IC because replacing a manager for 6-7 people was easier than replacing a tech lead for 35-40.
IC7 came while still a manager, credited to his technical vision: by then virtually the whole codebase was strict Hack, up from 5-10% of files in 2015.
Working with legends like type theorist Andrew Kennedy taught him not to fear pedigree and that accomplished seniors should stay humble and approachable to juniors.
He stayed ~14 years by refusing to make permanent decisions based on temporary circumstances: outside offers never fixed the actual (solvable) problem, and he consistently felt valued.
Advice to his younger self: when told to write code, ask how they knew it was the right code to write, and aim to be in the rooms deciding what to build.

quotes.speaker: Dwayne Reeves
quotes.quote: Writing code is just not what my job as a software engineer is. My job is to identify and solve problems.

quotes.speaker: Dwayne Reeves
quotes.quote: You're a rocket ship... I only cared about you getting us out of orbit. Now that we've done that, you should pass this off to someone else.

quotes.speaker: Dwayne Reeves
quotes.quote: I don't want to make permanent decisions based off of temporary circumstances.

quotes.speaker: Dwayne Reeves
quotes.quote: You should stop and think: how do they know that's the right code to write? You should aim to be the person who's making those decisions around what's the right thing to do.

nodes.label: GoogleX Chief Scientist On Imposter Syndrome, Career Growth, Project Taste | Carey Nachenberg
nodes.guest_role: GoogleX Chief Scientist, Symantec Fellow (IC10/VP-equivalent), UCLA professor
takeaways: Nachenberg rose from Symantec's first intern (1992, Peter Norton Group) to its senior-most engineer (Fellow, IC10/VP-equivalent) by repeatedly finding gaps: impactful projects the business needed that nobody was doing, like detecting polymorphic self-mutating viruses. Symantec never assigned him work; he picked all his own projects.

Symantec promoted to Distinguished Engineer and Fellow via a company-wide committee of technologists led by the CTO that reviewed portfolios quarterly for cross-division consistency; Nachenberg was made Fellow without even applying after the Veritas merger forced a level-set.

Top-level IC hiring is network-driven: Symantec's ex-COO pulled him into GoogleX with no application; the loop was ~8 interviews with no coding questions, mostly leadership discussion and sales pitches. Still, Google leveled him down from a Symantec L10-equivalent to L8 because 'we can't just hire fellows.'

Imposter syndrome kept him at Symantec 21+ years even after he stopped growing and being happy; only an external pull ('we want to interview you') got him to leave. His biggest regret is not leaving earlier — stay only as long as you're learning.

Intelligence matters less than communication, collaboration, and outcome focus: he knew a 200-IQ engineer stuck at L4 at Google due to poor communication and no business impact. Focusing on the company's/customer's outcomes and their metrics beats being smart.
At GoogleX/Chronicle he worked on Backstory, which ingested and indexed petabytes of enterprise security telemetry so threat-hunting queries that previously took 30 minutes ran in about two seconds.

At Lyft he drove the shift from hand-coded, hand-parametrized robotics driving algorithms to an end-to-end neural-network driver wrapped in a deterministic safety layer, achieved purely through influence with skeptical roboticists — then lost credit because he let others tell the story; document your accomplishments and claim specific, unembellished credit.

On speaking: design for the 30th-50th percentile listener, practice via optional lunchroom talks, and know people equate communication skill with intelligence — a single internal Stuxnet talk at X had colleagues calling him one of the smartest people they'd met.

He regrets letting students use LLMs on projects (project scores diverged sharply from exam understanding) and is banning them for projects while allowing them for learning; if AI reaches 'AGI-good' coding, the valuable engineering skills become outcome analysis, customer understanding, and communicating requirements — being a genuinely competent product manager.

Advice to younger self: don't let fear of failure hold you back, focus on outcomes and speak to your audience's needs rather than the technology, and find a manager who trusts you and gives you rope — a manager can make or break your career and happiness.

quotes.speaker: Carey Nachenberg
quotes.quote: If I have to be perfectly honest, I would say imposter syndrome... I stayed because it was comfortable and I complained. I complained all the time.

quotes.speaker: Carey Nachenberg
quotes.quote: Just because you have intelligence doesn't mean you're gonna be successful.

quotes.speaker: Carey Nachenberg
quotes.quote: People will think you're intelligent and they'll give you more credit and they'll introduce you to other opportunities based on your ability to communicate, because people associate that with intelligence.

quotes.speaker: Carey Nachenberg
quotes.quote: Don't let fear of failure hold you back. You probably can do more than you think you can do.

nodes.label: Meta Senior Staff Eng (IC7) On Zuck Stories, Rapid Career Growth, Code Machine Archetype
nodes.guest_role: Ex-Meta Senior Staff Eng (IC7) by 27, #1 code committer at Meta, co-founder of Formation
takeaways: Novati joined Facebook as an intern in May 2009 (~200 engineers), skipping a planned HCI PhD at University of Washington; he was a 'code machine' from day one, merging two competing internal task tools in his first week and announcing it to the whole company.

His raw code output stayed roughly constant his whole career; what got him to IC7 by 27 was accumulated judgment and taste — knowing which parts of the codebase are sensitive, how changes affect other teams' in-flight migrations, and when speed is safe.

Signature IC7-scale wins were relentless solo migrations no team would have finished: manually refactoring away ~5-6,000 'Preparable' framework classes over months, and eliminating every legacy direct-hit PHP endpoint in favor of the routing framework.

Trust is the code machine's currency: after burning credibility with a rushed bug, he publicly told the push team 'if this breaks the site, I will resign' — seriously — to force his own triple-checking and rebuild trust with the teams that shipped his code.

As IC7 he spent ~30% of focus on his team and ~70% on codebase-wide initiatives, nominally reporting to junior managers while effectively reviewed by his skip and meeting a VP monthly; he aggressively declined meetings and standups to protect coding time.

Zuck stories: he built emoji-reactions-on-any-post with Zuckerberg at a 2009 hackathon (code too bad to ship, but the idea became the industry norm), and during the 2010 Google Plus lockdown a company-wide bet got Zuck to personally merge a PR in exchange for Novati giving up office ripsticking.

His popular internal newsletter (Weekly NTI) predicted a South San Francisco office, but he now calls it a mistake: off-the-cuff posts about second-class 'non-software-engineer' titles and which companies were poaching engineers forced executives into damage control — though Zuckerberg personally encouraged him to keep posting.

On LLMs: they are the Vim-to-VSCode leap repeated; he produces ~5x more code in Formation's ~500k-line codebase by prompting for code he already sees in his head. Near-term AI amplifies experienced engineers with strong taste; long-term, AI managing its own services behind API contracts may make human-readable code irrelevant.

Common traits in Meta's IC7+-only group regardless of archetype (code machine, specialist, fixer): extreme conscientiousness, attention to detail, and sharpness — groups moved through new technical proposals in minutes with rapid precise follow-ups.
His self-admitted weakness: he prioritized problems whose solutions he could already see in his head over the org's stated top priority, which he believes capped his impact and frustrated managers.

He left Meta after ~10 years when all-in mutual loyalty faded into a business relationship and vesting logistics meant an ~80% take-home pay drop; he estimates career growth was 50/50 luck (landing at 2009 Facebook) versus hard work, the only factor in your control.

Advice: stop treating feedback as a grade. The growth loop is: do something, get feedback from people whose taste you aspire to (not peers barely ahead of you), and genuinely action it — that cycle compounds exceptionally fast.

quotes.speaker: Michael Novati
quotes.quote: More than once I've said this needs to get in and if it breaks the site, I will resign.

quotes.speaker: Michael Novati
quotes.quote: That accumulation of judgment and taste is what crosses the line to the E7.

quotes.speaker: Michael Novati
quotes.quote: Talent is something out of your control. And luck is outside of your control... hard work is the one thing that's within your control.

quotes.speaker: Michael Novati
quotes.quote: I was accepting feedback as a grade to judge myself and put pressure on myself to get a hundred percent next time, but I wasn't putting pressure on myself to actually improve.

nodes.label: 26 Year Old Meta Staff Eng (IC6) On Promotions, Redefining Expectations, Secret Equity Bonuses
nodes.guest_role: Meta Staff Engineer (IC6) at age 26; New Grad (IC3) to Staff in 3 years
takeaways: Simon Kindström went from New Grad (IC3) to Staff (IC6) at Meta in 3 years by age 26, earned the top 'Redefines Expectations' rating twice, and received discretionary equity ('secret bonuses').

His redefines-expectations work came from persistence through experimental failure: when experiments produced unexpected bad results he investigated root causes, modified, and reran them until he understood the failure or improved the results.

Writing well was a major career lever: write about your accomplishments and put the impactful, interesting parts at the top — engineers commonly bury the lede in technical details their audience finds irrelevant.

His manager recruited him by explaining the business impact of the work and how it would lead to career growth; the work then drove multiple significant revenue wins for Meta, which converted directly into top ratings and accelerated promotions.
His manager was central: coaching him closely throughout, with deep organizational knowledge and personal experience of rapid advancement before moving into management.

The critical Staff-level skill was scaling himself; his delegation failure mode was abdicating ownership of others' outcomes and scrambling at review time — the fix was 'delegate, don't abdicate': keep accountability while empowering others to execute.
He tried management as a deliberate experiment, found it pulled him away from the technical work he loved amid organizational thrash, and transparently switched back to the IC track.

quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman (show notes)
quotes.quote: He didn't give up on those experiments until he understood why.

quotes.speaker: Simon Kindström (via show notes)
quotes.quote: It's important to put the impactful, interesting parts of the writing at the top.

quotes.speaker: Simon Kindström (via show notes)
quotes.quote: Delegate, don't abdicate.

nodes.label: Instagram Principal Engineer (IC8) on Promotions, Breaking Prod, Tech Leading | Jake Bolam
nodes.guest_role: Principal Engineer (IC8) at Instagram
takeaways: On diff review Jake biases toward 'how can I accept this diff' rather than 'how can I reject this diff'; if a diff isn't risky or touching a critical path he prioritizes speed and gives a rudimentary review, pushing the envelope on project speed unless told he's moving too fast.

His promotion to IC8 came from a migration that grew from multi-team scope into moving all of Instagram's backend to an entirely new backend, affecting hundreds of teams; the blast radius plus success despite the risk earned the promotion.

His advice to his younger self is 'go where you're valued': he struggled after joining Meta on a team that didn't value his frontend skillset, and his career took off once he moved to an Instagram team that did.
He works about 40 hours a week even at IC8 thanks to systems built over his career, chiefly blocking out focus time and minimizing meetings far more than the average engineer.
His note-taking system is a mass of unorganized plain text files in VS Code, navigated by remembered keywords and cross-linked like a personal Wikipedia knowledge base.

quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman (summarizing Jake Bolam)
quotes.quote: He talked about biasing towards 'how can I accept this diff' rather than 'how can I reject this diff.'

quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman
quotes.quote: The blast radius of this work and project success despite the risk earned him a promotion to IC8.

nodes.label: OpenAI & Meta Distinguished Engineer (IC9) On Working With Zuck, Carmack & Career Growth | Philip Su
nodes.guest_role: Distinguished Engineer (IC9) at Meta, later OpenAI; ex-Microsoft
takeaways: What distinguished Mark Zuckerberg was his constant devotion to growth; John Carmack was one of the most impressive engineers Su worked with because of his prolific engineering ability — he could drop into a codebase he knew nothing about and give excellent feedback.

Su reached IC9 by founding and scaling Facebook's London office from around a dozen engineers to 500+, which gave him the blast radius to have IC9 impact; IC9 expectations are defined by the blast radius you have and what you can guarantee the success of.

The highest-level promotions are not a checklist of quantitative points; they run on qualitative feedback, needing the judgment of someone at a higher level to explain what makes the person's work special.

Su switched between IC and EM six times; for generalists there is a lot of commonality between management and tech leadership, which let him switch roles based on business need and personal enjoyment.
He eventually requested a demotion from IC9 to IC7 because he wanted to do more technical work; it relieved the pressure of IC9 expectations and preserved role-switching flexibility.
Generalists are much more effective at smaller companies; once companies get larger, specialists have more potential since they can go as deep as they want.
To write well, you need to read examples of good writing and take inspiration from it to improve your own.

quotes.speaker: Philip Su (via host summary)
quotes.quote: To write well, you need to read examples of good writing.

quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman
quotes.quote: Growing the team there from around a dozen engineers to 500+ gave him the blast radius to have IC9 impact.

nodes.label: Industry Secrets We Wish We Knew Before Graduating | Staff Eng Talk @ UCLA
nodes.guest_role: Staff Engineer at Google by 28; co-speaker with host Ryan Peterman at a UCLA UPE talk
takeaways: Luck influences promotions, but you can expand your 'luck surface area' by taking initiative to seek new opportunities and being prepared when they arrive.

Perfect code isn't enough: the best engineers weren't necessarily the top programmers from college but had balanced technical and soft skills — influencing others, collaborating in teams, and writing concisely.
People decide promotions, so you must advocate for yourself; visibility is an important part of growing your career alongside doing quality work.
Both speakers would choose Big Tech over startups straight out of college for instant credibility and foundational skills, then pivot to startups later with brand equity and expertise.
Lines of code != impact: the industry rewards results, not effort; writing less code while achieving greater impact is preferable.
Big Tech won't make you crazy rich: climbing levels is a reliable path to financial stability and freedom, but exceptional wealth requires breakout startup opportunities.
Job hopping helps early in a career, but promotions beyond Senior/Staff depend on influence and credibility that take time to build at each company, making late-career hopping counterproductive.
LLMs are productivity tools like the many non-AI tools engineers already use; SWE jobs look secure for roughly five years, with longer-term impacts unpredictable.

For college recruiting, resume projects are the minimum; persistence and joining smaller companies early accelerates experience. For internship return offers, finish well-defined projects quickly with high-quality code and propose improvements afterward.

quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman
quotes.quote: There's so much that no one taught me in college that I had to learn in industry myself.

quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman
quotes.quote: If that part isn't good, then most people won't watch the whole thing.

quotes.speaker: Article text
quotes.quote: Visibility is therefore an important part of growing your career.

nodes.label: 21x Hackathon Winner Turned College Dropout | Jia Chen Interview
nodes.guest_role: 21x hackathon winner, college dropout, co-founder of Sprint.dev, content creator
takeaways: Winning hackathons comes down to team dynamics, judge interaction, and smart project execution, not stacking credentials or showing off a complicated tech stack.
Pick hackathon teammates on skills and drive rather than credentials: past projects, GitHub activity, and ambition beat resumes; Jia has won with determined community-college students.
Keep hackathon presentations short and let judges interact with the project; teams that spend the time talking about their tech stack often lose.
Splitting into separate frontend and backend teams and merging late usually fails at hackathons; connect both early and iterate together.
Hackathons double as recruiting pipelines: recruiters at larger hackathons scout future interns and fast-track candidates for internship applications.
From a non-target school, make yourself the outlier: take leadership roles, spearhead big projects, and build a personal brand.
Develop 'agency' - the ability to take action and think on your feet - plus a 'get it done and move on' time-efficiency mindset.
College provides structure if you don't know what to learn but isn't necessary; for Jia it eventually dulled her creativity and passion for learning.
Don't drop out on a whim: set preconditions first by building projects, testing the idea, and evaluating your technical skills and investor network.
As a founder, move fast and continuously ship while avoiding burnout, and stay open to connections and learning opportunities that don't seem immediately useful.

quotes.speaker: Jia Chen
quotes.quote: Agency - the ability to take action and think on your feet.

quotes.speaker: Jia Chen
quotes.quote: Get it done and move on.

nodes.label: Amazon Principal Engineer On Layoffs, Interviewing & Career Growth | Steve Huynh
nodes.guest_role: Ex-Amazon Principal Engineer (~19 years), creator of the A Life Engineered YouTube channel
takeaways: Coding and system design tests just get you in the door; behavioral interviews determine leveling and cultural fit, so articulate your experience to match the target level.
Junior engineering roles face limited demand while Senior+ roles see extraordinary demand, and competition concentrates in pre-screening rather than the interviews themselves.
Amazon has an explicit up-and-out policy for SDE1s - promotion within a timeframe or termination - which relaxes at higher levels.
In Huynh's observation, Amazon layoffs typically hit people lacking cultural fit rather than poor performers.
The Principal Engineer promotion requires nearly two levels' worth of contribution simultaneously, with conflicting feedback along the way, so start preparing early.
Huynh's promotion project was overhauling Prime Video's outdated catalog infrastructure, a high-leverage system relied on by hundreds of dependent engineers.
To avoid layoffs, communicate directly with your manager about expectations; early transparent feedback prevents surprises.
Job-hop frequently early in your career for rapid advancement, but favor stability once you reach Senior+ where growth requires sustained tenure.
Huynh regrets not switching teams earlier and focusing too much on people-pleasing and external expectations.

quotes.speaker: Steve Huynh
quotes.quote: These tests just get you in the door.

nodes.label: Staff Engineer @ Meta by Age 25 | Evan King
nodes.guest_role: Ex-Meta Staff Engineer (IC6 by age 25), later startup founder
takeaways: Evan went from Junior (IC3) to Staff (IC6) at Meta in three years, reaching Staff by age 25.
Both Evan and host Ryan identify as 'code machines' - high-output engineers rather than natural prodigies; Evan started programming late in high school.
His manager handed him borderline engineering-management responsibilities to scale a growing team, and soft skills from founding Cornell's Hacking Club proved instrumental.
Impact trumps technical complexity: his Staff promotion came from a simple project with extraordinary results, not technical sophistication.
He was promoted in his first half by pursuing projects outside his core scope, showing agency in seeking opportunities.
His Senior-to-Staff case came from leading the creation of Meta's real-time integrity team after the Christchurch incident, combining visibility and impact.
He argues startups promote faster technical growth than big tech: big tech offers depth, startups provide breadth.
An after-hours crypto side project gained 10k+ users and eventually led him to leave big tech for entrepreneurship.
He regrets over-prioritizing work early on at the cost of friendships and self-reflection, and taking promotion shortcuts that sacrificed deep technical understanding he later needed.

quotes.speaker: Evan King
quotes.quote: Startups promote faster technical growth than big tech does.
